coredata.dc:description: Because of its high selectivity and ease of use, fluorescent probes are widely used in the fields of environmental monitoring, biomedicine and ion detection. As the research on fluorescent probes gradually deepens, all kinds of substances can be used as fluorescent probes in various fields. As a new type of material, nanomaterials have been widely used in medical, electronic, testing and food industries due to their excellent physical and chemical properties as well as biological properties. The focus of this review is to briefly introduce the properties of metal nanoparticles in nanomaterials as well as carbon quantum dots and the progress in their detection as fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence probes and their sensing applications in nanomaterials system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Because of its high selectivity and ease of use, fluorescent probes are widely used in the fields of environmental monitoring, biomedicine and ion detection. As the research on fluorescent probes gradually deepens, all kinds of substances can be used as fluorescent probes in various fields. As a new type of material, nanomaterials have been widely used in medical, electronic, testing and food industries due to their excellent physical and chemical properties as well as biological properties. The focus of this review is to briefly introduce the properties of metal nanoparticles in nanomaterials as well as carbon quantum dots and the progress in their detection as fluorescent probes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of people's living standard, the economy as well as the development of science and technology has become more and more rapid. The advantages of fluorescent probes, such as high sensitivity, high selectivity, and visualization without radiation, have led to their widespread use in various fields. Fluorescent probes are rapidly developing in many fields such as biology, chemistry, and pharmacology, and have significant advantages such as high efficiency, good sensitivity, and high selectivity in detection and imaging, while for the design of fluorescent probes follow a variety of fluorescence mechanisms, including excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT), internal charge transfer (ICT), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), aggregation-induced emission (AIE), etc. [1]. Fluorescent probes are typically designed by combining analyte recognition fragments with appropriate fluorophores. Various fluorophores with different excitation and emission wavelengths are used in the design of fluorescent probes, such as using organic molecules, transition metal complexes, metal nanoclusters, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as fluorophores [2].
Nanomaterials (NMs) are materials that are in at least one dimension in three dimensions at the nanometer size (1–100 nm) or are composed of them as basic units.

Nanomaterials have unique thermal, mechanical, electronic and biological properties. Their high surface-to-volume ratios, potential for surface functionalization and good thermal properties provide the flexibility needed for a wide range of analytical applications [3], and they have promising applications in biomedical and tissue engineering [4]. Nanomaterials are widely used in various aspects of life and industry. They can be used for functionalized film decontamination [5], as fuel additives or drug carriers, etc. [6,7]. Nanomaterials can be divided into inorganic nanomaterials [8], organic nanomaterials [9], metallic nanomaterials [1], nanoclay materials [10] and carbon nanomaterials [11].

NMs can be classified into different groups based on different criteria. In general, NMs are classified according to their dimension, morphology, state and chemical composition, and generally this classification depends on their dimensions. And based on their size and the overall shape of these materials, nanomaterials can be further classified into the following four categories. Zero-dimensional nanomaterials (0D) have all the dimensions of the nanoscale, i.e., less than 100 nm, such as quantum dots. One-dimensional nanomaterials (1D) are materials that have a one-dimensional non-nanometer scale while the other two dimensions are nanometer scale, such as metals, polymers, ceramics, and other substances. Two-dimensional nanomaterials (2D) contain only one dimension at the nanoscale, such as thin films, nanoplates, and nanocoatings. Three-dimensional (3D) nanomaterials are available in various sizes over 100 nm [12,13], such as foams, fibers, nanotubes, etc. Compared to 2D and 3D nanomaterials, 1D nanomaterials have the best selectivity and sensitivity for gases in the low concentration range due to their miniaturized structure, while 2D and 3D nanomaterials also have good sensing properties compared to bulk semiconductor materials [14]. In this review, several nanomaterials are briefly described, focusing on the application of metal nanoparticles in metallic nanomaterials and carbon quantum dots in carbon nanomaterials for detection purposes.

Metal NPs such as gold, iron, silver and other metal NPs, these materials have excellent chemical, optical and electrical properties. Metal NPs are easy to prepare, their surfaces are relatively easy to modify, and most are of low toxicity [15]. The size of some metal NPs depends on the design procedure, for example, by reducing the particle size to the nanoscale, the band gap of the electronic structure changes to discrete electronic levels and a large number of atoms appear on the surface. As the nanometer size decreases, these atoms on the surface material become more active due to the increased distance between the atomic coordinates and the unsaturated sites [16].

Bimetallic nanoparticles (BMNPs) are composite materials composed of two metals with unique properties characterized by the properties and performance of different metals, unique chemical stability and reactivity, and size-dependent electrical and optical properties, and are widely used due to their unique properties. These properties are usually influenced by composition, shape, and size. The preparation method has a significant effect on the size distribution of BMNPs and their recyclability as catalyst materials. Several bimetallic materials such as Al-TiO2-Ag, Ag-Cu, Au/Pd, Au - Pd@SiO2, Fe-Cu and Ag-Au have shown that in addition to optical and magnetic properties, the combination of the two metals can show different NPs dispersion, stability and surface activity [16].

Metal nanoparticles are metal nanomaterials. With the advancement of science and technology, metal nanoparticles have been widely used in medical, pharmaceutical, food and agricultural applications such as drug delivery, targeted cancer therapy, biomarker localization, gene delivery and molecular imaging due to their attractive optical, electronic, biological and catalytic properties [17]. For example, nanoparticles can be induced to merge into solids at relatively low temperatures and usually do not melt, and the wavelength of nanoparticles is below the critical wavelength of light, which makes them transparent, a property that makes them widely used in cosmetics, coatings and packaging; Similarly, the application of metal nanoparticles in the medical field is reflected in the fact that metal nanoparticles can be attached non-destructively to single-stranded DNA, and that nanoparticles can cross the vascular system and localize any target organ [18]. Not only that, metal nanoparticles also have unique optical properties, and the more common precious metal nanoparticles in metal nanoparticles due to their interaction with incident light will produce the unique optical properties. The localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) phenomenon is obtained due to the interaction of metal nanostructures with incident light, which affects the optical properties of metal nanoparticles [19]. And the shape of metal nanoparticles [19,20], metal nanoparticles prepared from different kinds of metals [21], and the number of metals in metal nanoparticles, such as bimetallic nanoparticles have better optical properties than monometallic nanoparticles [22], all affect the optical properties of metal nanoparticles. Photoluminescence (PL) is the light emitted by a material under light excitation, and there is no systematic discussion on PL of metal nanoparticles, while PL of noble metal nanoparticles among single metal nanoparticles, such as AuNPs, is more common, and crystal structure, valence, and surface structure can affect its PL [23,24] and some bimetallic nanoparticles are more commonly discussed [25].

The common precious metal nanoparticles are mainly gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), which have unique chemical and physical properties and are widely used in various fields, such as diagnosis and treatment of cancer in the medical field [26], and the research on them has been very mature. A large number of ligands have been used to stabilize gold/silver nanoparticles in recent years [27]. AuNPs have received much attention due to their unique optical and electronic properties, and it has been found that AuNPs can be successfully used for detection and diagnosis [28], biosensing, and other fields [29]. In addition, AuNPs are widely used in optical sensors due to their surface plasmon resonance properties. With a variety of properties such as catalysis, magnetic and photopolarization, electrical conductivity, microbial activity and enhanced Raman scattering, AgNPs are used in a wide range of applications due to their unique physicochemical properties [30].

The more common of the common metal nanoparticles are copper nanoparticles (CuNPs), and iron nanoparticles (FeNPs). CuNPs are cheaper and more readily available compared to other MNPs, are easily mixed and combined with other metals, ceramics and polymers, and exhibit physicochemical stability in the compounds; FeNPs have a higher specific surface area and can oxidize and reduce a wide range of pollutants and are now often used to remediate contaminated water [31]. In addition, there are iridium nanoparticles [32] and ruthenium nanoparticles [33], etc. Among the monometallic nanoparticles (MNPs), except for some precious metal nanoparticles, other MNPs still have a very promising development.

As research on metal nanoparticles continues, researchers have discovered bimetallic nanoparticles, which also have excellent chemical properties. Compared to monometallic nanoparticles, the reduced size of bimetallic nanoparticles increases the surface-to-volume ratio, thereby increasing the number of adsorbed surface active sites and lowering the energy barrier, thereby increasing catalytic activity [34]. The current research development of BMNPs is more promising compared to MNPs.

Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have unique properties and play an important role in several scientific fields. Carbon is a solid isotope that comes in various forms such as graphite, amorphous carbon and diamond. These CNMs consist of sp2 carbon atom hybridization, and the nanoscale CNMs have different chemical and physical properties, including electrical conductivity, mechanical properties, chemical stability, and thermal properties. Therefore, the wide application of CNMs has attracted a lot of attention. According to the morphology of CNMs, they can be divided into the following categories: (a) Fullerenes (0D), which are isomers of carbon (C);(b) Carbon nanotubes (1D), which is a material with a hollow structure made of carbon atoms connected in a hexagonal structure; (c) Graphene sheets (2D), which are sp2-bonded carbon structures forming a hexagonal or honeycomb lattice; (d) Graphite and nanodiamond (3D), graphite is a hexagonal arrangement of sp2 carbon atoms, while nanodiamond is laminated spherical [14,16,35]. Currently, CNMs also offer the possibility of combining with other types of nanomaterials to form nanocomposites [36].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as carbon dots (CDs) or carbon nanodots, are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescence properties, consisting of ultrafine, dispersed, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm. Since its discovery, it has been widely studied due to its unique photoluminescent properties, good photoluminescent charge transfer properties, good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and low toxicity, and has shown great promise for applications in the fields of photocatalysis, drug carriers [37], optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, ion detection, etc. [38]. It is worth mentioning that CQDs also have unique optical properties. One very attractive feature of CQDs is their large fluorescence emission intensity, which arises from the quantum confinement (QCE) that occurs when the exciton's Bohr is larger than the average size of CQDs. When the size of CQDs decreases, the HOMO (highest occupied orbital)-LUMO (lowest unoccupied orbital) gap increases, leading to photon emission in the UV region. Another relevant optical property of CQDs is their chemiluminescence, which is produced in chemical reactions in which intermediate radicals break down into electronically excited radicals and deactivate themselves [39]. CQDs are known to have highly luminescent as well as multicolor luminescence properties, while CQDs exhibit two types of photoluminescence: (a) surface photoluminescence and (b) core (i.e., quantum) photoluminescence, CQDs solutions show solventdependent and wide-FWHM photoluminescence spectra, which indicates that the photoluminescence properties are more from the surface than from the core [40]. As for the PL properties of CQDs, the fundamental reason for CQDs to exhibit PL is the complex of electron-hole pairs in the local sp2 clusters, which can modulate the PL of CDs by adjusting the size of conjugated π-domains, which is considered as the intrinsic PL center [37]. The significant emission feature of CQDs is that their PL spectra are mainly dependent on the excitation wavelength, and they are color shifted with the red shift of the excitation wavelength (above 400 nm) and also decrease in intensity due to the low light absorption in the visible range. The PL properties of CQDs can be tuned by changing the surface state, changing the synthesis route, doping with heteroatoms, choosing different precursors and other methods [41]. For the preparation of CQDs, there are top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down methods imply etching CQDs from carbon targets or exfoliating CQDs from carbon nanoparticles, including laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation and arc discharge methods; The bottom-up approach is to first obtain carbon precursors by combustion, heating or microwave methods, and then prepare CQDs by chemical oxidation, microwave-assisted methods or hydrothermal methods [42]. And CQDs can be prepared quickly and easily by using cheap and available natural materials (e.g. milk, honey, lemon, etc.) as green carbonaceous precursors [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of metal nanoparticles for detection

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their excellent physical and chemical properties, heavy metals are widely used in industry, agriculture and other fields. However, heavy metal pollution is inevitable in the process of mass production and emission. Due to the non-degradable nature of heavy metal ions, they can cause irreversible harm to humans and other organisms even at low levels of exposure and ingestion. In the environment and the organism, the lack or overload of metal ions may cause environmental pollution or collective dysfunction [44], and the more common methods that exist for detecting heavy metal ions microatomic emission spectrometry, graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, etc. These methods are accurate, but costly. Therefore, a method similar to the detection of heavy metal ions with metal nanoparticles is both accurate and economical.

We briefly introduced the excellent properties of AuNPs and AgNPs above, and this part will briefly introduce some of the applications of AuNPs and AgNPs in the detection of ions. Due to their unique physicochemical properties, AuNPs have a wide range of applications in detecting ions. For example, Bindhu et al. prepared AuNPs by mixing cannabis extracts as a reducing agent with HAuCl4 and used it to detect Fe3+ in water [45]. Not only can AuNPs be used alone to detect ions, but also by adding certain small molecule modifications to encapsulate AuNPs for ion detection. For example, Zhang et al. mixed trisodium Citrate with chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), then functionalized AuNPs with 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA), and used it to detect Cr3+.This method interacts with the prepared AuNPs through covalent bonds in the 4-MBA, and the functionalized AuNPs aggregate in the presence of Cr3+ ions through ionic template chelation, leading to significant color changes that can be detected by the naked eye as well as by UV–Vis spectroscopy to determine the detection of Cr3+ [46]. Similarly, a cysteamine functionalized AuNPs (Cyta-AuNPs) was proposed in the study of Li et al. The AuNPs prepared by this method are rich in amino groups, well dispersed in aqueous media, and can be used for the detection of Cu2+ [47]. In the study by Yang et al., bromine-coated AuNPs were prepared by mixing an aqueous solution of HAuCl4.H2O with a toluene solution of tetran-octylammonium (TOBA) followed by the addition of NaBH4 and used as a probe for the detection of Pb2+, and TOBA was used in this method so that the bromine ion was involved in the reaction with Pb2+, thus achieve a sensitive detection of Pb2+ [48].

Due to the unique physical and chemical properties of AgNPs, the study of their detection has been widely discussed. For example, in Alzahrani's study, a method was proposed to prepare green AgNPs from onions and use them for the detection of Hg2+ [49]. Similar to AuNPs, AgNPs can also be modified by small molecules and other substances and used for assays and other tasks. For example, in the study by Akram et al. an Epicatechin-coated ECAgNPs was proposed and used for the detection of Pb2+. In the presence of Pb2+, the reaction of Epicatechin with Pb2+ led to a sudden extinction in absorbance intensity, resulting in enhanced absorbance intensity of ECAgNPs [50]. Similarly, in the study by Ullah et al. a Bispyra-AgNPs with Bispyrazolone modified AgNPs was proposed and used for the colorimetric detection of Cu2+ [51].

Not only two metals, gold and silver, can be used as metal nanoparticles, but there are also many other metals that can be used as metal nanoparticles for assays. Such as antimony nanoparticles (SbNPs), Nunes et al. prepared SbNPs and modified graphene oxide (GO) for the detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in chamomile tea by preparing graphene oxide in an ethanol base and then dropping SbCl3 solution into graphene oxide to prepare nanomaterials of SbNPs combined with reduced graphene oxide [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal nanoparticle detection of small molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have a wide range of applications in various aspects due to their various advantages. Therefore, metal nanoparticles also perform well in detecting small molecules. melamine is commonly used in the manufacture of chemicals, industrial products and medical materials, but if ingested in excess of safe limits melamine can cause kidney failure and even death in infants. Therefore, it is extremely important for its highly sensitive detection. A probe 7-(benzylamino)−9,9-dibutyl-9H-fluorene-2-carbaldehyde (BDFC) coupled to AuNPs for the detection of melamine was proposed in the study of Lu et al. The probe was prepared by adding HAuCl4 to citric acid as a raw material for AuNPs. The detection mechanism is that when BDFC is mixed with AuNPs, BDFC can adsorb on the surface of AuNPs, which leads to the fluorescence intensity sudden extinction of BDFC by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and after adding melamine, BDFC can be released due to the competition of AuNPs adsorption between melamine and BDFC, so that the fluorescence intensity of BDFC can be recovered (as in Fig. 1 ) [53]. Similarly, a composite nanomaterial prepared from AuNPs and amino acids was also proposed in the study of Sharma et al. for tyrosine-reducing GNPs as peroxidase nanoparticles, Ky2 aptamer as a biosensor method for the detection of kanamycin [54].

In the study of Tuana et al. a method was proposed to prepare AuNPs@Thr based on AuNPs modified with tetramethylthiuram disulfide (thiram) and used for colorimetric detection of TNT and tetryl. The characteristic surface plasmon resonance band (SPR) of AuNPs@Thr increases with TNT/tetryl concentration due to the rapid aggregation of the amine group of thiram with the donor-acceptor (D-A) interaction between TNT and nitro-groups in tetryl. As the concentration of TNT/tetryl increases, the color of the solution changes from red to purple to blue, thus achieving sensitive detection of TNT and tetryl [55].

Similarly, AgNPs have a wide range of applications and good working results in the detection of small molecules. Dopamine (DA), one of the catecholamine neurotransmitters, helps cells transmit impulses and plays an important role in the function of the central nervous and cardiovascular systems [56]. Therefore, a fluorescence detection method based on DNA-mediated detection of DA by AgNPs was mentioned in Lin et al. This fluorescence sensing method consists of DNA-templated silver nanostructures and the intercalating dye Genefinder (GF), which is weakly fluorescent but exhibits significant fluorescence enhancement upon binding to double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). In the absence of DA, AgNPs were tightly but non-covalently bound to dsDNA, which blocked the binding of the DNA-binding intercalating dye GF, so the fluorescence was sudden extinction, whereas after the addition of DA, dsDNA was replaced by DA from the silver surface and the fluorescence intensity of dsDNA-mediated AgNPs was restored [57].

Metal nanoparticles are widely used to design different types of biosensors for colorimetric analysis, fluorescence analysis, luminescence analysis, etc. due to their easy synthesis and easy binding to thiols and other substances. Fluorescence-based sensor methods are important in the detection of large molecules such as tumor markers, nucleic acids, proteins, carbohydrates and antibodies [58].

Among many metal nanoparticles, AuNPs are the most attractive nanoparticles in the field of nanobiotechnology because of their special physicochemical properties such as strong adsorption, no photobleaching and scintillation, easy synthesis, simple coupling chemistry, and good biocompatibility [59]. In a study by Abhijith et al., cedar leaf extract was used as raw material AuNPs and used for the detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), whose fluorescence was sudden extinction by AuNPs after the addition of AFB1 [59]. We mentioned above that it is more common to take certain small molecules to modify AuNPs and use them for assay work. For example, in Ömer et al. study, a AuNPs@(4MP-MAA)/Fe(III) based on AuNPs modified with 4-mercaptophenol (4MP) and mercaptoacetic acid (MAA) as binders and Fe3+ was proposed for the colorimetric determination of desferrioxamine (DFO). After the addition of DFO, the shape and intensity of the SPR of AuNPs@(4MP-MAA)/Fe(III) changed due to the interaction of Fe3+ with DFO, and the color changed from burgundy to purple as the nanoparticles aggregated (as in Fig. 2 ) [60].

The use of aptamers coupled to AuNPs for use in the assay field is now also widely discussed. It used an ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheet (g-C3N4NS) and used it as a fluorescent probe. g-C3N4NS interacted with unlabeled aptamer/AuNPs by FRET to detect DGX. The aptamer/AuNPs coupling was added to a suspension of g-C3N4NS containing an appropriate amount of NaCl, and sufficient overlap occurred between the surface plasmon resonance spectrum of AuNPs and the emission spectrum of g-C3N4NS, so that the fluorescence was sudden extinction. However, after the addition of DGX to the solution, the fluorescence of g-C3N4NS was restored due to aptamer/DGX binding and aggregation of AuNPs in the presence of salt (Fig. 3 ) [61].

In the same study by Elahi et al. a composite nanomaterial is proposed to be combined with DNA probes and used for medical purposes, based on AuNPs and ferromagnetic nanoparticles (MNP) with a fluorescence sensing method for the detection of Shigella multi-Spa genes. It used citric acid reduction to synthesize AuNPs, and then prepared amino-functionalized MNPs by a single-step process without insertion of gas in an autoclave by polyol technology to immobilize DNA probes on the surface of AuNPs and MNPs. Fluorescent DNA probe1 on the surface of AuNPs serves as a signal reporter, and other DNA probes on the surface of AuNPs and MNPs serve as ligands and isolators for target DNA. Fluorescent DNA Probe2- AuNPs-DNA Probe1 and DNA Probe3- MNP were added to the target DNA to form fluorescent DNA Probe1- AuNPs-DNA Probe1- Target DNA-DNA Probe3- MNPs complexes. The AuNPs on the release surface of this complex were separated with a magnetic field and a fluorescent DNA probe, and then the fluorescence intensity of the released fluorescent DNA probe2 was measured (Fig. 4 ) [58], this method combines metal nanoparticles with DNA for sensitive detection of pathogens. It follows that the integration of nanoparticles with biological systems has become possible for future diagnostic work by combining the intrinsic properties of nanoparticles and immobilizing specific ligands, such as oligonucleotides and proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon quantum dots for detection
Carbon quantum dots detect metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, regarding the application of CQDs in the detection of ions, the detection of Fe3+ is the most common, and different precursors are generally selected to prepare CQDs. Iron is the central metal ion of the hemoglobin group of hemoglobin, which plays a vital role as a cofactor in various enzymatic reactions. Iron deficiency may lead to various blood disorders such as anemia, decreased blood pressure and decreased immunity [62]. In a study by Suresh et al., fluorescent CQDs were prepared from tomatoes and used to detect Fe3+ with good sensitivity, and the addition of Fe3+ solution to the prepared CQDs solution resulted in significant fluorescence sudden extinction [63]. For example, in a study by Fang et al. using soy flour as precursor, CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method to detect Fe3+, and CQDs were sudden extinction in the presence of Fe3+ [64], and Yang et al. used honey as raw material and prepared CDs for Fe3+ detection using a hydrothermal method, which also resulted in fluorescence sudden extinction in the presence of Fe3+ [65]. Also in the study of Zhang et al. CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using black sesame as raw material and used as a highly fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ [66].

Not only food can be used as a carbon source to prepare CQDs, but also biomass can be used as a carbon source to prepare CQDs. For example, in a study by Yang et al., CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using corn straw as raw material and used for sensitive detection of Cu2+, and their fluorescence was significantly sudden extinction in the solution of CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ [67], use of corn stover as a carbon source also contributes to the environment to a certain extent. Similarly, in the study of Rooj et al. Cu2+ was also detected, using Tuberose Polianthes tuberosa L. as a carbon source and using a green and economical method to prepare CQDs and for the sensitive detection of Cu2+ as well as Fe2+, two ions that can sudden extinction the fluorescence of CQDs [68]. Similarly, Song et al. used cellulose as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the modifier to prepare CQDs by hydrothermal method and used it to detect Cr3+ [69]. In the study by Luo et al., 3,4-Dihydroxy-L-phenylalanine (levodopa) was used as the carbon source to prepare fluorescent CQDs for the detection of Cu2+ by microwave-assisted method, so that the fluorescence of CQDs was statically sudden extinction [70]. A fluorescent probe method using ammonium citrate, ethylenediamine and sodium hydroxide as precursors to prepare CDs and used to detect Hg2+ was proposed in the study by Cai et al. the fluorescence of CDs was sudden extinction after the addition of Hg2+ [71].

The preparation of CQDs from foodstuffs and the detection of different ions are also numerous. In the study by Arumugam et al., western bluebells were used as precursors to prepare CQDs and used to detect Ag+. When Ag+ was added, energy transfer between Ag+ and oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs formed chelate complexes, resulting in strong sudden extinction of CQD luminescence [72]. In the study by Hoan et al. a hydrothermal method using lemon juice as a precursor was proposed to prepare CQDs and use them as fluorescent probes for the detection of molybdenum (Mo6+), a heavy metal ion of traditional Chinese medicine that is widely used in industry and is extremely important for its detection. This method apparently sudden extinction the fluorescence of CQDs in the presence of Mo6+ [73]. In another study by Hoan et al., CQDs were also prepared by hydrothermal method using lemon juice as a precursor and used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Vanadium (V5+) [74]. Similarly, in a study by Gao et al. carbon dots (CDs) with red fluorescence were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and neutral red as raw materials to detect Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+, and the CDs fluorescence was significantly sudden extinction after adding the above heavy metal ions to the CDs [75].

Not only CQDs can be used to detect ions, but CQDs with doped elements are also extremely widely used, such as nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [76,77]and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) [78] are more common. N-CQDs exhibit excellent performance in terms of electrocatalytic activity as well as detection. In Zhao et al.'s study, N-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using chitosan as the carbon and nitrogen source and used for the detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples. When Fe3+ was added, the electron-deficient Fe3+ complexes with functional groups (such as carboxyl and amino groups) on the surface of N-CQDs led to the splitting of the d-orbital of Fe3+ and the partial transfer of electrons from the excited state of N-CQDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+, resulting in the inhibition of electron leap in the radiative form (FL emission), which led to the sudden fluorescence extinction [79]. Similarly, in the study by Gu et al. N-CQDs were prepared for the detection of Fe3+, only the precursors were different. In this method, Lycium barbarum, which has an extensive plant area and is rich in nitrogen, was chosen as the precursor and hydrothermal method was used to prepare N-CQDs, which resulted in sudden fluorescence extinction in the presence of Fe3+ [80]. A similar application is found in the study of Luo et al. who proposed N-CDs prepared by hydrothermal method using 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine (TAPI) as precursor, which were selected and used as fluorescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ [81]. And the value of S-CQDs for heavy metal detection is also very attractive. In Wang et al.'s study, S-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid as the carbon source and sodium sulfide (Na2S) as the sulfur source and used to detect Hg2+. It was found that Hg2+ could induce fluorescence burst of S-CQDs, possibly due to the high affinity between mercury and sulfur, which affected the electron transfer on the surface of S-CQDs, increasing the probability of non-radiative electron leap and reducing the fluorescence, or due to the complexation reaction of mercury with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S-CQDs, which aggregated S-CQDs, increasing the non-radiative electron leap and reducing the fluorescence intensity [82].

Since the discovery of the excellent properties of CQDs, people started to explore the properties of CQDs doped with elements or semiconductors. Therefore, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N/S-CQDs) were prepared and used for the detection of Fe3+ in the study of Deng et al. The method uses sulfonamide acid as raw material to prepare N/S-CQDs by hydrothermal method. When Fe3+ binds to it, the affinity of Fe3+ is enhanced by organic groups such as amino and sulfonic acid groups on the surface of N/S-CQDs, and Fe3+ carries 3 positive charges, which can cause more electrons to leap to 3d orbitals, resulting in sudden fluorescence extinction [83]. Similarly, in Li et al.'s study, N/S-CQDs were prepared from citric acid, urea and L-cysteine for sensitive detection of Hg2+, which resulted in sudden fluorescence extinction of N/S-CQDs in the presence of Hg2+ [84]. Of course, there are also quantum dots doped by many other elements, for example, in Omer's study, glucose was chosen as the carbon source, phosphoric acid as the (H3PO4) phosphorus source and polyethylene glycol diamine as the nitrogen source to prepare nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon quantum dots (N/P-CQDs) and used to detect Cu2+, and after the addition of Cu2+, the sudden fluorescence extinction [85]. In summary, whether N-CQDs or S-CQDs or N/S-CQDs and other metal-doped CQDs, the fields of application are broad and sensitive, and the detection ion applications mentioned in this section are only the tip of the iceberg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dot detection of small molecules
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are not only used to detect ions, but also have a wide range of applications in the detection of small molecules. In Lin et al.'s study, a microwave-assisted method was proposed to prepare CQDs using L-cysteine (L-cys) and citric acid as raw materials for the detection of DA, and after adding DA to CQDs, the N-H bond of CQDs reacted with the DA structure, in which the DA structure acted as an electron acceptor to cause the electron transfer between CQDs and DA, resulting in the sudden extinction of fluorescence of CQDs [86]. Similarly, in a study by Yang et al. a fluorescent probe method for the detection of DA was proposed for CQDs prepared by hydrothermal method using L-arginine as raw material, and the fluorescence was significantly sudden extinction after the addition of DA [87]. Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin, is an essential water-soluble vitamin and plays an important role in human growth, metabolism and development. In the study of Zhang et al. mentioned above, the detection of Fe3+ was followed by the detection of AA. The fluorescence of CQDs was burst after the addition of Fe3+, and the fluorescence of CQDs was restored after the addition of AA on this basis [66]. Glutathione (GSH) is present in humans and animals and plays an important role in the human defense system. In the study of Cai et al. we mentioned above, Hg2+ was assayed, and on this basis, GSH was assayed. When GSH was added to the CDs-Hg2+ system, the fluorescence was restored due to the stronger binding capacity of GSH to Hg2+ than to CDs and Hg2+, releasing CDs to allow recovery of fluorescence [71].

In recent years, with the increasing level of scientific research, N-CQDs have also been used for the detection of small molecules. For example, in the study of Yadav et al. a hydrothermal method was used to prepare N-CQDs using neem leaves as a carbon and nitrogen source and for the sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as well as AA, which used tetramethylbenzedine (TMB) as a chromogenic substrate. In the presence of TMB (N-CQDs + TMB), the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CQDs was slightly sudden extinction, while in the presence of H2O2 (N-CQDs + TMB + H2O2), so that its emission intensity was completely complete sudden extinction, indicating that TMB was completely oxidized. In the presence of AA (N-CQDs + TMB + H2O2 + AA), the oxidation -TMB was reduced to TMB, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs(as shown in Fig. 5 ) [88]. Similarly, while N-CQDs for the detection of small molecules of dopamine, one of the neurotransmitters we mentioned above, were prepared in the study of Zhao et al. The hydrothermal method was used to prepare the desired N-CQDs using polyacrylamide as the carbon and nitrogen source, and the fluorescence of the N-CQDs was sudden extinction after the addition of DA [89]. And in the study of Ömer et al. proposed a citric acid (CA) as precursor, ethylenediamine (EDA) and diaminocyclohexane (DACH) as nitrogen source, and a reflux method for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), the fluorescence of N-CQDs was significantly sudden extinction in the presence of TNT [90]. It can be seen that the technology of CQDs for the detection of small molecules is already very mature, and the detection applications we have mentioned in this paper are only a small part of the CQDs detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots in the medical field for the detection of ions, small molecules and other applications
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs have been widely used in cell imaging, in vivo imaging, and catalysts because of their simple preparation, high quantum yield, and good biocompatibility. Its excellent optical properties make it very promising in fluorescence detection, and in recent years, the research has gradually applied it to the detection in the medical field. However, there are relatively few studies on the application of CQDs alone to the detection of various viruses in medical aspects, while the application of combining CQDs with aptamers, amino acids, metal nanoparticles [91] and other substances for detection in medical fields is more maturely developed. For example, in Wang et al.'s study, a new fluorescent probe aptamer conjugated carbon quantum dot complex (CDs-apt) was developed and used to detect Salmonella typhimurium by hydrothermal preparation of CDs using citric acid as the carbon source and binding the CDs to the aptamer. CDs-apt fluorescence appeared significantly enhanced after the addition of S. typhimurium [92]. A similar study was presented in Thakur et al. using CQDs coupled to poly-l-lysine to detect bacterial lipopolysaccharide endotoxins [93].

Among the other element-doped CQDs, there are also many that can be used in the medical field for detection purposes. N-CQDs, which we mentioned above, are already a very mature technology application in detecting ions. N-CQDs can be used to detect ions alone or by combining other substances or co-doping with other elements to detect the target ions. However, when N-CQDs are used to detect bacteria and viruses in the medical field, the combination of N-CQDs with aptamers or antibiotics is often used as a new fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of bacteria. For example, an N-CQDs with blue fluorescence based on alcohol-soluble amphotericin B-modified corn stover was proposed in the study by Yu et al. and the prepared N-CQDs@AmpB were used to detect candida albicans, and the fluorescence was significantly enhanced after the addition of candida albicans [94].

In this paper, we briefly describe some of our research on the use of metal nanoparticles in metal nanomaterials and carbon quantum dots in carbon nanomaterials for the detection of ions and small molecules and for the detection of target ions and small molecules in the medical field. The development and research of both in ion detection and detection of small molecules are well established and widely used, but there is still much room for development in the use of CQDs and doped N-CQDs alone for medical detection. This review can provide some help to readers who want to understand some of the applications of nanomaterials in fluorescence detection.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ping Lu is currently postgraduate student at the Inner Mongolia Normal University with Professor Jun Ai. She is currently focused on research into bionanomaterials and biosensor construction.

Jun Ai obtained his BSc and MS degree in Chemistry in 2000 and 2004, respectively, from Inner Mongolia Normal University and Inner Mongolia University. He then worked in Inner Mongolia Normal University and at present he is a professor. In 2009, he joined Prof. Erkang Wang's group, and received his PhD degree in analytical chemistry in January 2013 from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His studies are centered on biosensor construction and fabrication of functional nanobiomaterials.
